texts.orig: Random Reed-Solomon Codes and Random Linear Codes are Locally Equivalent
texts.text: Random Reed-Solomon Codes and Random Linear Codes are Locally Equivalent

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We establish an equivalence between two important random ensembles of linear codes: random linear codes (RLCs) and random Reed-Solomon (RS) codes. Specifically, we show that these models exhibit identical behavior with respect to key combinatorial properties-such as list-decodability and list-recoverability-when the alphabet size is sufficiently large.
texts.text: We establish an equivalence between two important random ensembles of linear codes: random linear codes (RLCs) and random Reed-Solomon (RS) codes. Specifically, we show that these models exhibit identical behavior with respect to key combinatorial properties-such as list-decodability and list-recoverability-when the alphabet size is sufficiently large.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We introduce monotone-decreasing local coordinate-wise linear (LCL) properties, a new class of properties tailored for the large alphabet regime. This class encompasses list-decodability, list-recoverability, and their average-weight variants. We develop a framework for analyzing these properties and prove a threshold theorem for RLCs: for any LCL property P , there exists a threshold rate R P such that RLCs are likely to satisfy P when R < R P and unlikely to do so when R > R P . We extend this threshold theorem to random RS codes and show that they share the same threshold R P , thereby establishing the equivalence between the two ensembles and enabling a unified analysis of list-recoverability and related properties.
texts.text: We introduce monotone-decreasing local coordinate-wise linear (LCL) properties, a new class of properties tailored for the large alphabet regime. This class encompasses list-decodability, list-recoverability, and their average-weight variants. We develop a framework for analyzing these properties and prove a threshold theorem for RLCs: for any LCL property P , there exists a threshold rate R P such that RLCs are likely to satisfy P when R < R P and unlikely to do so when R > R P . We extend this threshold theorem to random RS codes and show that they share the same threshold R P , thereby establishing the equivalence between the two ensembles and enabling a unified analysis of list-recoverability and related properties.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Applying our framework, we compute the threshold rate for list-decodability, proving that both random RS codes and RLCs achieve the generalized Singleton bound. This recovers a recent result of Alrabiah, Guruswami, and Li (2023) via elementary methods. Additionally, we prove an upper bound on the list-recoverability threshold and conjecture that this bound is tight. Our approach suggests a plausible pathway for proving this conjecture and thereby pinpointing the list-recoverability parameters of both models. Indeed, following the release of a prior version of this paper, Li and Shagrithaya (2025) used our equivalence theorem to show that random RS codes are near-optimally list-recoverable.
texts.text: Applying our framework, we compute the threshold rate for list-decodability, proving that both random RS codes and RLCs achieve the generalized Singleton bound. This recovers a recent result of Alrabiah, Guruswami, and Li (2023) via elementary methods. Additionally, we prove an upper bound on the list-recoverability threshold and conjecture that this bound is tight. Our approach suggests a plausible pathway for proving this conjecture and thereby pinpointing the list-recoverability parameters of both models. Indeed, following the release of a prior version of this paper, Li and Shagrithaya (2025) used our equivalence theorem to show that random RS codes are near-optimally list-recoverable.

texts.orig: 1 Introduction
texts.text: 1 Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An (error-correcting) code of length n is a set C of words over some fixed alphabet Σ. When Σ is the finite field F q (for some prime power q ) and C is a linear subspace of F n q , we say that C is a linear code . The rate of C is log | Σ | | C | n , which equals dim C n when C is linear. The distance of a linear code C is min x ∈C\{ 0 } wt( x ), where wt( x ) is the Hamming weight , which is the number of non-zero entries in x .
texts.text: An (error-correcting) code of length n is a set C of words over some fixed alphabet Σ. When Σ is the finite field F q (for some prime power q ) and C is a linear subspace of F n q , we say that C is a linear code . The rate of C is log | Σ | | C | n , which equals dim C n when C is linear. The distance of a linear code C is min x ∈C\{ 0 } wt( x ), where wt( x ) is the Hamming weight , which is the number of non-zero entries in x .

texts.orig: Using our characterization of R P , we compute the threshold rate for list-decodability (and its average-weight variant), showing that RLCs approach the generalized Singleton bound [GST22], provided that the alphabet size q is at least 2 Ω ( L 2 ) . This recovers a recent result from [AGL23], via an elementary approach that does not rely on external results such as the GM-MDS theorem [DSY14; Lov21; YH19b] employed in [AGL23]. Additionally, we show that the threshold rate for ( ρ, /lscript,L )-list-recoverability is at most ( 1 -1 log /lscript ( L +1) )( 1 -L +1 L +1 -/lscript · ρ ) + o (1) and conjecture this bound to be tight. 4 This upper bound was shown in a recent prior work by [CZ24] for (Folded) Reed-Solomon codes, who also conjectured that this bound is tight for that code model.

This recovers a recent result from [AGL23], via an elementary approach that does not rely on external results such as the GM-MDS theorem [DSY14; Lov21; YH19b] employed in [AGL23]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The second part of this paper focuses on random RS codes . Our main result in this part is a threshold theorem for LCL properties in the random RS model. We show that a threshold exists for any such property P , and remarkably, this threshold is equal to R P ! This means that P is likely to hold for a random RS code if and only if is likely to hold for an RLC of similar rate, provided the alphabet size q is sufficiently large. We thus say that these two code models are equivalent for LCL properties or locally equivalent .
texts.text: The second part of this paper focuses on random RS codes . Our main result in this part is a threshold theorem for LCL properties in the random RS model. We show that a threshold exists for any such property P , and remarkably, this threshold is equal to R P ! This means that P is likely to hold for a random RS code if and only if is likely to hold for an RLC of similar rate, provided the alphabet size q is sufficiently large. We thus say that these two code models are equivalent for LCL properties or locally equivalent .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: An immediate conclusion of this equivalence is that random RS codes and RLCs share the same list-recoverability parameters. For instance, to prove that a random RS code of rate R is ( ρ, /lscript,L ) -list-recoverable, it essentially suffices to establish the same for an RLC, and vice versa. Moreover, our characterization makes it possible to compute the relevant threshold R P , yielding results applicable to both code models simultaneously.
texts.text: An immediate conclusion of this equivalence is that random RS codes and RLCs share the same list-recoverability parameters. For instance, to prove that a random RS code of rate R is ( ρ, /lscript,L ) -list-recoverable, it essentially suffices to establish the same for an RLC, and vice versa. Moreover, our characterization makes it possible to compute the relevant threshold R P , yielding results applicable to both code models simultaneously.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Prior works such as [BGM23; GZ23; AGL23] have proved that random RS codes also approach the generalized Singleton bound by using a technique first introduced in [BGM23], which made use of an equivalence between the GMS-MDS theorem and list-decoding for random RS codes. Combining the list-decodability result for RLCs from the first part of our work with the equivalence result between random RS codes and RLCs in the second part, we show that random RS codes also approach the generalized Singleton bound. This effectively recovers the main result of the prior works without relying on the GM-MDS theorem.
texts.text: Prior works such as [BGM23; GZ23; AGL23] have proved that random RS codes also approach the generalized Singleton bound by using a technique first introduced in [BGM23], which made use of an equivalence between the GMS-MDS theorem and list-decoding for random RS codes. Combining the list-decodability result for RLCs from the first part of our work with the equivalence result between random RS codes and RLCs in the second part, we show that random RS codes also approach the generalized Singleton bound. This effectively recovers the main result of the prior works without relying on the GM-MDS theorem.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our result about the local equivalence between random RS codes and RLCs, besides being interesting in its own right, provides a definite answer to the connection between the two wellstudied code models, which had been hinted at in previous works. Furthermore, our result enables researchers to establish findings regarding local properties in one code model and directly transfer them to the other, allowing them to work with their preferred code model.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.2 Local Code Properties and the LCL Framework
texts.text: 1.2 Local Code Properties and the LCL Framework

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our notion of LCL properties extends a general framework, initially introduced in [MRRSW20], for studying list-decodability and list-recoverability as part of a broader class of code properties. A set of words in F n q that all lie within the same Hamming ball of radius ρn is called ρ -clustered . A set of words X ⊆ F n q is said to be ( ρ, /lscript ) -recovery-clustered if there exists a sequence of sets Z 1 , . . . , Z n ⊆ F q , each of size at most /lscript , such that for every x ∈ X , the condition |{ i ∈ [ n ] | x i / ∈ Z i }| ≤ ρn holds. Thus, a code C is ( ρ, L ) -list-decodable (or ( ρ, /lscript,L ) -list-recoverable) if and only if it does not contain a ρ -clustered (or ( ρ, /lscript ) -recovery-clustered) set of size L +1. We interpret a clustered (or recovery-clustered) set of codewords in C as a witness to the code's non-list-decodability (or non-list-recoverability). This leads to the observation that list-decodability and list-recoverability have short witnesses, drawing a natural analogy to co-NP languages or Π 1 logical formulas.
texts.text: Our notion of LCL properties extends a general framework, initially introduced in [MRRSW20], for studying list-decodability and list-recoverability as part of a broader class of code properties. A set of words in F n q that all lie within the same Hamming ball of radius ρn is called ρ -clustered . A set of words X ⊆ F n q is said to be ( ρ, /lscript ) -recovery-clustered if there exists a sequence of sets Z 1 , . . . , Z n ⊆ F q , each of size at most /lscript , such that for every x ∈ X , the condition |{ i ∈ [ n ] | x i / ∈ Z i }| ≤ ρn holds. Thus, a code C is ( ρ, L ) -list-decodable (or ( ρ, /lscript,L ) -list-recoverable) if and only if it does not contain a ρ -clustered (or ( ρ, /lscript ) -recovery-clustered) set of size L +1. We interpret a clustered (or recovery-clustered) set of codewords in C as a witness to the code's non-list-decodability (or non-list-recoverability). This leads to the observation that list-decodability and list-recoverability have short witnesses, drawing a natural analogy to co-NP languages or Π 1 logical formulas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In [MRRSW20], this viewpoint was used to study list-decodability and list-recoverability within a newly defined class of (monotone-decreasing) local code properties . A property is said to be local if it has short witnesses, like clustered sets for list-decodability. The main finding of [MRRSW20] is that Gallagher codes , a random ensemble of LDPC codes, possess local properties similar to those of RLCs, meaning they are just as list-decodable and list-recoverable (with high probability). Later, [GM22] extended this framework to show that randomly punctured low-bias codes also share local properties with RLCs. These results can be seen as reductions between code ensembles. 5
texts.text: In [MRRSW20], this viewpoint was used to study list-decodability and list-recoverability within a newly defined class of (monotone-decreasing) local code properties . A property is said to be local if it has short witnesses, like clustered sets for list-decodability. The main finding of [MRRSW20] is that Gallagher codes , a random ensemble of LDPC codes, possess local properties similar to those of RLCs, meaning they are just as list-decodable and list-recoverable (with high probability). Later, [GM22] extended this framework to show that randomly punctured low-bias codes also share local properties with RLCs. These results can be seen as reductions between code ensembles. 5

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The reduction results in [MRRSW20] and [GM22] stem from the threshold theorem for local properties for RLCs [MRRSW20, Theorem 2.8]. This theorem states that for any local property P , there is a threshold rate R P such that RLCs with a rate below R P are likely to satisfy P , whereas those with a rate above R P are unlikely to do so. An analogous theorem for plain random codes is proven in [GMRSW22].
texts.text: The reduction results in [MRRSW20] and [GM22] stem from the threshold theorem for local properties for RLCs [MRRSW20, Theorem 2.8]. This theorem states that for any local property P , there is a threshold rate R P such that RLCs with a rate below R P are likely to satisfy P , whereas those with a rate above R P are unlikely to do so. An analogous theorem for plain random codes is proven in [GMRSW22].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The threshold theorem crucially relies on the property P having short witnesses. For instance, if P is the property of ( ρ, L )-list-decodability, the witnesses are ρ -clustered sets of size L +1. To apply the theorem, subsets of F b q (where b = L +1) are classified into types, and the theorem uses the expected number of these witnesses in a code C to determine whether C is likely to satisfy P . However, the number of types grows exponentially in q b , which is manageable for fixed q but becomes unwieldy when q increases with n . For a more formal treatment of this technique, see [MRSY24].
texts.text: The threshold theorem crucially relies on the property P having short witnesses. For instance, if P is the property of ( ρ, L )-list-decodability, the witnesses are ρ -clustered sets of size L +1. To apply the theorem, subsets of F b q (where b = L +1) are classified into types, and the theorem uses the expected number of these witnesses in a code C to determine whether C is likely to satisfy P . However, the number of types grows exponentially in q b , which is manageable for fixed q but becomes unwieldy when q increases with n . For a more formal treatment of this technique, see [MRSY24].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This exponential growth in the number of types motivates the need for a refined approach. Our framework of LCL properties avoids explicitly enumerating types and can handle cases where q is large, often keeping the complexity independent of q (see Remark 3.3).
texts.text: This exponential growth in the number of types motivates the need for a refined approach. Our framework of LCL properties avoids explicitly enumerating types and can handle cases where q is large, often keeping the complexity independent of q (see Remark 3.3).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Notably, random RS codes arise as random puncturings of the full RS code RS F q ( F q ; k ), which is known to have low bias, suggesting that the results of [GM22] could apply to their analysis. However, the methods developed in [MRRSW20; GM22] are tailored to codes over relatively small alphabets and do not extend to RS codes, where q ≥ n is required. Our LCL framework fills this gap by extending the local properties approach to the large-alphabet regime.
texts.text: Notably, random RS codes arise as random puncturings of the full RS code RS F q ( F q ; k ), which is known to have low bias, suggesting that the results of [GM22] could apply to their analysis. However, the methods developed in [MRRSW20; GM22] are tailored to codes over relatively small alphabets and do not extend to RS codes, where q ≥ n is required. Our LCL framework fills this gap by extending the local properties approach to the large-alphabet regime.

texts.label: footnote
texts.orig: 5 Note that [MRRSW20; GM22] only prove one-sided reductions, transferring monotone-decreasing local properties from RLCs to other ensembles. In contrast, the reduction in the present paper goes both ways.
texts.text: 5 Note that [MRRSW20; GM22] only prove one-sided reductions, transferring monotone-decreasing local properties from RLCs to other ensembles. In contrast, the reduction in the present paper goes both ways.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Informally, a property P is b -LCL if it is witnessed by a small set of codewords x 1 , . . . , x b ∈ F n q such that, for each 1 ≤ i ≤ n , the vector ( x 1 i , . . . , x b i ) satisfies certain linear constraints. For example, ρ -clusteredness is characterized by a large number of equality constraints (which are, in particular, linear) among x 1 i , . . . , x b i for many coordinates 1 ≤ i ≤ n . We provide a formal treatment in Section 2.3.
texts.text: Informally, a property P is b -LCL if it is witnessed by a small set of codewords x 1 , . . . , x b ∈ F n q such that, for each 1 ≤ i ≤ n , the vector ( x 1 i , . . . , x b i ) satisfies certain linear constraints. For example, ρ -clusteredness is characterized by a large number of equality constraints (which are, in particular, linear) among x 1 i , . . . , x b i for many coordinates 1 ≤ i ≤ n . We provide a formal treatment in Section 2.3.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As mentioned, the LCL framework encompasses list-decodability and list-recoverability, along with their average-weight variants. It also naturally captures other properties, such as average pairwise distance (defined in [CGV13]) and list-decodability from burst errors (e.g., [RV09]) under a unified lens.
texts.text: As mentioned, the LCL framework encompasses list-decodability and list-recoverability, along with their average-weight variants. It also naturally captures other properties, such as average pairwise distance (defined in [CGV13]) and list-decodability from burst errors (e.g., [RV09]) under a unified lens.

texts.orig: 1.3.1 List-Decodability and List-Recoverability of RS Codes
texts.text: 1.3.1 List-Decodability and List-Recoverability of RS Codes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In light of the importance of RS codes, it is a major open problem to explicitly construct RS codes with good list-decodability or list-recoverability parameters. Currently, no non-trivially listdecodable or list-recoverable explicit RS codes are known. For list-decodability, this means that known explicit RS codes are only list-decodable up to the Johnson bound ([Sud97], [GS98]). A negative list-decodability result for the full RS code RS F q ( F q ; k ) in some parameters regimes is given in [BKR09].
texts.text: In light of the importance of RS codes, it is a major open problem to explicitly construct RS codes with good list-decodability or list-recoverability parameters. Currently, no non-trivially listdecodable or list-recoverable explicit RS codes are known. For list-decodability, this means that known explicit RS codes are only list-decodable up to the Johnson bound ([Sud97], [GS98]). A negative list-decodability result for the full RS code RS F q ( F q ; k ) in some parameters regimes is given in [BKR09].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lacking explicit constructions, much attention has been given to the corresponding existential problems. List-decodability of random RS codes was studied in [RW14; ST20; GLSTW21a; FKS22; GST23]. Finally, in [BGM23; GZ23; AGL23] it was shown that random RS codes are list-decodable up to capacity, 7 and, furthermore, that they achieve the Elias bound 8 and the generalized Singleton bound [ST20]. [BGM23] noted an interesting connection between the GM-MDS theorem (proven in [Lov21; YH19a]) and list-decoding of RS codes, which allowed them to prove that random RS code can achieve the Generalized Singleton bound, but with exponential field size. In [BDG24] the authors prove that in order to achieve the Generalized Singleton bound exactly, exponential field size is in fact necessary. However, [GZ23] circumvent this by showing that it is possible to approach the bound arbitrarily closely with only quadratic field size, which was improved to linear by the work of [AGL23]. Recent works have generalized these results to Algebraic-Geometric codes [BDGZ24], other polynomial based code ensembles [BDG23], and also to Gabidulin codes in the rank metric [GXYZ24]. We note that [BGM23; GZ23; AGL23; BDG23; BDGZ24; GXYZ24] all utilize a common framework that relies on the GM-MDS theorem, or a variant of it as a crucial component. It is not yet clear whether this framework can be extended to deal with other local properties, such as list-recovery.

It does not reflect the subsequent contribution of [LS25], discussed in Section 1.1, which established a new result on the list-recoverability of RLCs in the large alphabet regime and, using our main theorem, significantly advanced the known bounds for list-recoverability of random RS codes.

texts.text: 7 A family of codes is list-decodable up to capacity if it achieves ( ρ, L )-list-decodability with rate 1 -h q ( ρ ) -ε for arbitrarily small ε and L ≤ poly( n ).

texts.orig: 8 A family of codes achieves the Elias bound if it satisfies ( ρ, L )-list-decodability with rate 1 -h q ( ρ ) -ε for arbitrarily small ε and L ≤ O ( 1 ε ) . This is a stricter requirement than achieving list-decoding capacity. Essentially, codes achieving the Elias bound are at least as list-decodable as plain random codes. See [MRSY24].

The first is a non-trivial list-recovery result for random RS codes, where the rate decreases to zero as n grows, while the second shows the existence of random RS codes of rate Ω ( ε √ /lscript · log 1 /ε ) that are (1 -ε, /lscript,O ( /lscript/ε ))-list-recoverable. The third result shows that random RS codes are ( ρ, /lscript,L ) -list-recoverable with rate approaching 1 -ρ /lscript + ρ .

The first is a non-trivial list-recovery result for random RS codes, where the rate decreases to zero as n grows, while the second shows the existence of random RS codes of rate Ω ( ε √ /lscript · log 1 /ε ) that are (1 -ε, /lscript,O ( /lscript/ε ))-list-recoverable. The third result shows that random RS codes are ( ρ, /lscript,L ) -list-recoverable with rate approaching 1 -ρ /lscript + ρ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We mention that for explicit Folded Reed-Solomon (FRS) codes, there have been a series of recent works [KRSW18; Tam23; Sri24; CZ24] which prove positive list-decodability and listrecoverability results for these codes. In particular, [Tam23] proved that explicit FRS codes having relative distance δ are ( δ -ε, /lscript,L )-list-recoverable with L ≤ O ( /lscript ε O ( 1+log /lscript ε ) ) . For the setting of list-decoding, [CZ24] proved that the codes meet the generalized Singleton bound, and thus are (1 -R -ε, L )-list-decodable with L ≤ O (1 /ε ). Additionally, they also prove a negative result for the list-recoverability of Folded Reed-Solomon codes, showing that every Folded Reed-Solomon code of rate R having distinct evaluation points cannot be (1 -R -ε, /lscript, /lscript R 2 ε -1 -1) list-recoverable. This result holds for every constant folding parameter s ≥ 1, and hence also holds for Reed-Solomon codes having distinct evaluation points.
texts.text: We mention that for explicit Folded Reed-Solomon (FRS) codes, there have been a series of recent works [KRSW18; Tam23; Sri24; CZ24] which prove positive list-decodability and listrecoverability results for these codes. In particular, [Tam23] proved that explicit FRS codes having relative distance δ are ( δ -ε, /lscript,L )-list-recoverable with L ≤ O ( /lscript ε O ( 1+log /lscript ε ) ) . For the setting of list-decoding, [CZ24] proved that the codes meet the generalized Singleton bound, and thus are (1 -R -ε, L )-list-decodable with L ≤ O (1 /ε ). Additionally, they also prove a negative result for the list-recoverability of Folded Reed-Solomon codes, showing that every Folded Reed-Solomon code of rate R having distinct evaluation points cannot be (1 -R -ε, /lscript, /lscript R 2 ε -1 -1) list-recoverable. This result holds for every constant folding parameter s ≥ 1, and hence also holds for Reed-Solomon codes having distinct evaluation points.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.3.2 List-Decodability and List-Recoverability of RLCs
texts.text: 1.3.2 List-Decodability and List-Recoverability of RLCs

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The ( ρ, L )-list-decodability of RLCs for a fixed L has been the focus of extensive research [ZP81; GHSZ02; GHK11; CGV13; Woo13; RW14; RW18; LW21; GLMRSW22; AGL23], employing at least four different methods. Collectively, these works demonstrate that RLCs achieve the Elias bound in the following settings:
texts.text: The ( ρ, L )-list-decodability of RLCs for a fixed L has been the focus of extensive research [ZP81; GHSZ02; GHK11; CGV13; Woo13; RW14; RW18; LW21; GLMRSW22; AGL23], employing at least four different methods. Collectively, these works demonstrate that RLCs achieve the Elias bound in the following settings:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · When q = 2 [GHSZ02; LW21].
texts.text: When q = 2 [GHSZ02; LW21].

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · When q ≤ O (1) and ρ is bounded away from q -1 q [GHK11].
texts.text: When q ≤ O (1) and ρ is bounded away from q -1 q [GHK11].

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · When q ≥ 2 Ω ( L ε ) , as shown in [AGL23] and in this work.
texts.text: When q ≥ 2 Ω ( L ε ) , as shown in [AGL23] and in this work.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The studies [CGV13; Woo13; RW14] provide positive results in the regime where ρ → q -1 q , though these do not achieve the Elias bound. A significant open problem remains: proving that RLCs meet the Elias bound across all parameter settings, ideally through a unified proof technique.
texts.text: The studies [CGV13; Woo13; RW14] provide positive results in the regime where ρ → q -1 q , though these do not achieve the Elias bound. A significant open problem remains: proving that RLCs meet the Elias bound across all parameter settings, ideally through a unified proof technique.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The study of list-recoverability for RLCs can be divided into the large q and small q regimes. For large q (e.g., when q is exponentially large in L ), the only known positive result comes from a straightforward application of the Zyablov-Pinsker Lemma [ZP81], which provides a weak lower bound on the threshold rate that rapidly diminishes as q or L increase. In this work, we establish the upper bound R P ≤ ( 1 -1 log /lscript ( L +1) )( 1 -L +1 L +1 -/lscript · ρ ) and conjecture that this bound is tight. This is the same bound that was first proven in a recent prior work by [CZ24] for ( ρ, /lscript,L )-listrecoverability of (Folded) Reed-Solomon codes. Importantly, our result indicates that RLCs do not reach an analogue of the Elias bound for list-recovery in the large q regime, meaning they are not as list-recoverable as plain random codes.
texts.text: The study of list-recoverability for RLCs can be divided into the large q and small q regimes. For large q (e.g., when q is exponentially large in L ), the only known positive result comes from a straightforward application of the Zyablov-Pinsker Lemma [ZP81], which provides a weak lower bound on the threshold rate that rapidly diminishes as q or L increase. In this work, we establish the upper bound R P ≤ ( 1 -1 log /lscript ( L +1) )( 1 -L +1 L +1 -/lscript · ρ ) and conjecture that this bound is tight. This is the same bound that was first proven in a recent prior work by [CZ24] for ( ρ, /lscript,L )-listrecoverability of (Folded) Reed-Solomon codes. Importantly, our result indicates that RLCs do not reach an analogue of the Elias bound for list-recovery in the large q regime, meaning they are not as list-recoverable as plain random codes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: List-recovery in the regime where /lscript ≥ q Ω(1) is studied in [RW14; RW18], which give positive results. However, [GLMRSW22] demonstrates that when q is a large power of a small prime, RLCs underperform significantly compared to plain random codes in this setting as well.
texts.text: List-recovery in the regime where /lscript ≥ q Ω(1) is studied in [RW14; RW18], which give positive results. However, [GLMRSW22] demonstrates that when q is a large power of a small prime, RLCs underperform significantly compared to plain random codes in this setting as well.

texts.orig: 1.4 Open Problems
texts.text: 1.4 Open Problems

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.4.1 List-Recovery and Non-Local Properties
texts.text: 1.4.1 List-Recovery and Non-Local Properties

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Determining the threshold for list-recovery in the large q regime remains a significant open problem, as it would reveal the list-recovery parameters of both random RS codes and RLCs. As noted earlier, we conjecture this threshold to be ( 1 -1 log /lscript ( L +1) )( 1 -L +1 L +1 -/lscript · ρ ) .
texts.text: Determining the threshold for list-recovery in the large q regime remains a significant open problem, as it would reveal the list-recovery parameters of both random RS codes and RLCs. As noted earlier, we conjecture this threshold to be ( 1 -1 log /lscript ( L +1) )( 1 -L +1 L +1 -/lscript · ρ ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another challenge arises in the small q regime, such as when q = poly( /lscript ). Our current methods cannot directly address list-recoverability in this setting due to the exponential dependence of q on locality. Relaxing this dependence is an important goal, as it could deepen our understanding of list-recovery in this regime.
texts.text: Another challenge arises in the small q regime, such as when q = poly( /lscript ). Our current methods cannot directly address list-recoverability in this setting due to the exponential dependence of q on locality. Relaxing this dependence is an important goal, as it could deepen our understanding of list-recovery in this regime.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Furthermore, extending our framework to analyze highly non-local properties would be valuable. Consider the following example: fix a large q and let 0 < R < 1, q (1 -R ) < /lscript < q , and ε > 0. Let P represent the property of (0 , /lscript,L )-list-recoverability for codes in F n q , where L = /lscript n · q -(1 -R ) n · (1+ ε ). Note that P is not a local property, since L is typically exponential in n , but it is a natural one to consider.
texts.text: Furthermore, extending our framework to analyze highly non-local properties would be valuable. Consider the following example: fix a large q and let 0 < R < 1, q (1 -R ) < /lscript < q , and ε > 0. Let P represent the property of (0 , /lscript,L )-list-recoverability for codes in F n q , where L = /lscript n · q -(1 -R ) n · (1+ ε ). Note that P is not a local property, since L is typically exponential in n , but it is a natural one to consider.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Notice that /lscript n · q -(1 -R ) n is the expected size of the set C ∩ Z 1 × Z 2 ×···× Z n , where C ⊆ F n q is a fixed code of rate (1 -R ), and Z 1 , . . . , Z n are independent uniformly random subsets of F q of size /lscript . Thus, a code C satisfies P if its maximum intersection with any product set Z 1 × Z 2 ×··· × Z n is at most (1 + ε ) times this expectation. From a pseudorandomness perspective, a code satisfying P can be said to fool product sets (also known as combinatorial rectangles) within a (1 + ε ) factor.

Thus, a code C satisfies P if its maximum intersection with any product set Z 1 × Z 2 ×··· × Z n is at most (1 + ε ) times this expectation. From a pseudorandomness perspective, a code satisfying P can be said to fool product sets (also known as combinatorial rectangles) within a (1 + ε ) factor.

texts.orig: Consider the question of whether any linear code satisfies this property. In particular, do RLCs satisfy it? Both questions were essentially answered in the affirmative by [MPSW21] when R > 1 2 and q is a large prime. For R ≤ 1 2 , these questions remain open. We conjecture that the answer is again positive, provided that q is a sufficiently large prime.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1.4.2 An Alphabet-Uniform Framework
texts.text: 1.4.2 An Alphabet-Uniform Framework

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As discussed in Remark 3.3, the LCL framework developed here is well-suited for sufficiently large alphabets. For instance, Corollary 3.6 on the list-decodability of RLCs applies when q ≥ 2 Ω ( L ε ) , where ε is the gap to capacity. In contrast, the classical local property framework from [MRRSW20] is designed for smaller alphabets. Our goal is to unify these approaches into a single framework that works uniformly across all alphabet sizes.
texts.text: As discussed in Remark 3.3, the LCL framework developed here is well-suited for sufficiently large alphabets. For instance, Corollary 3.6 on the list-decodability of RLCs applies when q ≥ 2 Ω ( L ε ) , where ε is the gap to capacity. In contrast, the classical local property framework from [MRRSW20] is designed for smaller alphabets. Our goal is to unify these approaches into a single framework that works uniformly across all alphabet sizes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Currently, RLCs are shown to achieve the Elias bound for list-decodability in two cases: when q is constant or when q ≥ 2 Ω ( L ε ) , leaving a gap between these two regimes. This gap highlights the differences between the two methods used to study local properties. The proof for the constant q case [GHK11] aligns well with the classical local properties framework, whereas our proof for the large q case (Corollary 3.6) is captured within the LCL framework. Developing a unified local property framework may provide a uniform proof that RLCs achieve the Elias bound across all alphabet sizes.
texts.text: Currently, RLCs are shown to achieve the Elias bound for list-decodability in two cases: when q is constant or when q ≥ 2 Ω ( L ε ) , leaving a gap between these two regimes. This gap highlights the differences between the two methods used to study local properties. The proof for the constant q case [GHK11] aligns well with the classical local properties framework, whereas our proof for the large q case (Corollary 3.6) is captured within the LCL framework. Developing a unified local property framework may provide a uniform proof that RLCs achieve the Elias bound across all alphabet sizes.

texts.orig: 1.4.3 Optimality of Random Linear Codes
texts.text: 1.4.3 Optimality of Random Linear Codes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the large q regime, RLCs and random RS codes are essentially optimally list-decodable, as they approach the generalized Singleton bound. We conjecture that this optimality extends more
texts.text: In the large q regime, RLCs and random RS codes are essentially optimally list-decodable, as they approach the generalized Singleton bound. We conjecture that this optimality extends more

texts.label: text
texts.orig: broadly within the class of linear codes and holds for all LCL properties (see Section 2.3 for a formal definition).
texts.text: broadly within the class of linear codes and holds for all LCL properties (see Section 2.3 for a formal definition).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Conjecture 1.1 (Optimality of RLCs and random RS codes for LCL properties) . For every ε > 0 and b ∈ N , there exist constants n 0 and q 0 such that the following holds: Let P be a b -local (monotone-decreasing) LCL property. If q ≥ q 0 and n ≥ n 0 , then any linear code in F n q with rate at least R P + ε does not satisfy P .
texts.text: Conjecture 1.1 (Optimality of RLCs and random RS codes for LCL properties) . For every ε > 0 and b ∈ N , there exist constants n 0 and q 0 such that the following holds: Let P be a b -local (monotone-decreasing) LCL property. If q ≥ q 0 and n ≥ n 0 , then any linear code in F n q with rate at least R P + ε does not satisfy P .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Following the original publication of this paper, [LS25] proved the conjecture for the case of list-recoverability. If correct, this conjecture would simultaneously generalize this negative result for list-recovery and the generalized Singleton bound.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2 Average-Weight List-Decodability and Average-Weight List-Recoverability
texts.text: 2.2 Average-Weight List-Decodability and Average-Weight List-Recoverability

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As mentioned in Section 1, list-decodability and list-recoverability have average-weight variants. We define them here.
texts.text: As mentioned in Section 1, list-decodability and list-recoverability have average-weight variants. We define them here.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 2.1. Let X ⊆ F n q be a set of words. If there exists some z ∈ F n q such that ∑ x ∈ X wt( x -z ) | X | ≤ ρn , we say that X is ρ -average-weight-clustered . If there exists a sequence of sets Z 1 , . . . , Z n ⊆ F n q , each of size at most /lscript , such that ∑ x ∈ X |{ i ∈ [ n ] | x i / ∈ Z i }| | X | ≤ ρn , we say that X is ( ρ, /lscript ) -average-weight-recovery-clustered .
texts.text: Definition 2.1. Let X ⊆ F n q be a set of words. If there exists some z ∈ F n q such that ∑ x ∈ X wt( x -z ) | X | ≤ ρn , we say that X is ρ -average-weight-clustered . If there exists a sequence of sets Z 1 , . . . , Z n ⊆ F n q , each of size at most /lscript , such that ∑ x ∈ X |{ i ∈ [ n ] | x i / ∈ Z i }| | X | ≤ ρn , we say that X is ( ρ, /lscript ) -average-weight-recovery-clustered .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A code is said to be ( ρ, L ) -average-weight list-decodable if it does not contain a ρ -average-weight-clustered set of size larger than L . A code is said to be ( ρ, /lscript,L ) -average-weight list-recoverable if it does not contain a ( ρ, /lscript ) -average-weight-recovery-clustered set of size larger than L .
texts.text: A code is said to be ( ρ, L ) -average-weight list-decodable if it does not contain a ρ -average-weight-clustered set of size larger than L . A code is said to be ( ρ, /lscript,L ) -average-weight list-recoverable if it does not contain a ( ρ, /lscript ) -average-weight-recovery-clustered set of size larger than L .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Note that the average-weight variants are stronger than the plain versions of these properties. Specifically, if a code is ( ρ, L ) -average-weight list-decodable, it is also ( ρ, L ) -list-decodable, and similarly for list-recoverability.
texts.text: Note that the average-weight variants are stronger than the plain versions of these properties. Specifically, if a code is ( ρ, L ) -average-weight list-decodable, it is also ( ρ, L ) -list-decodable, and similarly for list-recoverability.

texts.text: A property of ( q -ary length n ) codes is a family of codes P in F n q . For a code C ⊆ F n q , if C ∈ P , we say that C satisfies P .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The notion of b -local profiles can be regarded as a generalization of Intersection Matrices, originally introduced in [ST20] (see also [GZ23], which presents an equivalent concept under the name Reduced Intersection Matrices). The fundamental observation underlying these works is that any code that is not list-decodable must contain a subset of codewords that exhibit significant agreement with some fixed vector in the ambient space. Equivalently, these codewords must agree with one another on a substantial number of coordinates. [ST20] and [GZ23] leveraged this insight by representing codewords as columns of a matrix, where coordinate-wise agreements among codewords manifest as linear constraints on the matrix rows. (Reduced) Intersection Matrices serve as symbolic matrices that encode such agreement constraints. The concept of a b -local profile extends this framework by encoding constraints on coordinates in a more general manner, permitting the representation of linear constraints rather than merely agreement constraints. This generalization plays a crucial role in establishing the threshold theorem for RLCs and in demonstrating the equivalence between RLCs and random RS codes.
texts.text: The notion of b -local profiles can be regarded as a generalization of Intersection Matrices, originally introduced in [ST20] (see also [GZ23], which presents an equivalent concept under the name Reduced Intersection Matrices). The fundamental observation underlying these works is that any code that is not list-decodable must contain a subset of codewords that exhibit significant agreement with some fixed vector in the ambient space. Equivalently, these codewords must agree with one another on a substantial number of coordinates. [ST20] and [GZ23] leveraged this insight by representing codewords as columns of a matrix, where coordinate-wise agreements among codewords manifest as linear constraints on the matrix rows. (Reduced) Intersection Matrices serve as symbolic matrices that encode such agreement constraints. The concept of a b -local profile extends this framework by encoding constraints on coordinates in a more general manner, permitting the representation of linear constraints rather than merely agreement constraints. This generalization plays a crucial role in establishing the threshold theorem for RLCs and in demonstrating the equivalence between RLCs and random RS codes.

In particular, rather than directly studying listdecodability and list-recoverability, we investigate the complements of these properties.

texts.orig: In the classic local property framework (see [MRRSW20]), the number of types associated with a b -local property is equal to the number of ways to distribute n unlabeled balls between q b labeled bins, a term which grows exponentially in q . In the present LCL framework, | M | can be seen as analogous to the base n logarithm of the number of types. The fact that | M | can remain constant as q grows is crucial to the suitability of LCL properties for studying the large alphabet regime.

texts.orig: Remark 3.9. The divisibility conditions in Theorems 3.1 and 3.8 are not very significant, as the theorems provide nearly identical bounds even if the length n does not satisfy these conditions. Indeed, one can puncture the RLC C , removing a tiny fraction of its coordinates to obtain a shortened code D that satisfies the divisibility condition, allowing the theorem to be applied. Moreover, C and D differ only slightly in their list-decodability and list-recoverability parameters, so the result for D also gives a close bound for C .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For a fixed LCL property P , Theorem 3.10 reveals a threshold phenomenon for random RS codes. Specifically, P is highly likely to be satisfied when R > R P and almost certainly not satisfied when R < R P . The sharpness of this transition increases rapidly with q . Crucially, the threshold matches that of RLCs, implying that an RLC and a random RS code with similar rates are likely to satisfy the same local properties. Indeed, any positive result about local properties of RLCs can immediately be transferred to random RS codes, and vice versa. We illustrate this paradigm by the following corollary.

Thus, a tighter bound is obtained by considering the potential Φ ( V ′ , U ′ , R ) (here U ′ = F 1 q ), which we informally think of as implied by ( V , U ). As we show in the following threshold proposition , considering Φ ( V , U ) as well as Φ ( V ′ , U ′ ) for all ( V ′ , U ′ ) implied by ( V , U ), yields a necessary and sufficient condition for C to contain ( V , U ) with high probability.

texts.orig: Let us now revisit Example 4.2 in light of Proposition 4.3. In that example, consider the vector space W = { x ∈ F 2 q | x 1 = x 2 } . It is not difficult to see that Φ ( V , W, R ) = Rn , which is larger than Φ ( V , F 2 q , R ) = ( 2 R -1 2 ) n whenever R < 1 2 . Proposition 4.3 thus implies that an RLC of rate < 1 2 is unlikely to contain ( V , F 2 q ) . In Example 4.2 we reached a similar conclusion by projecting the rows of a matrix A ∈ M V , F 2 q onto their difference. The kernel of this projection F 2 q → F 1 q is exactly W . The proof of the first part of Proposition 4.3 generalizes this method. The second part of the proposition says that this method is essentially tight.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5 List-Decodability and List-Recoverability of RLCs
texts.text: 5 List-Decodability and List-Recoverability of RLCs

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this section we use the characterization of the threshold rate, given in Eq. (14), to compute the threshold for list-decodability and average-weight list-decodability of RLCs. We then prove an upper bound on the threshold for list-recoverability and average-weight list-recoverability of RLCs.
texts.text: In this section we use the characterization of the threshold rate, given in Eq. (14), to compute the threshold for list-decodability and average-weight list-decodability of RLCs. We then prove an upper bound on the threshold for list-recoverability and average-weight list-recoverability of RLCs.

texts.orig: We require some notation. Fix a ∈ N . A tuple of polynomials ( P 1 , . . . , P a ) ∈ F q [ X ] a is said to be k -bounded if deg( P i ) < k for every 1 ≤ i ≤ a . We denote the set of all k -bounded tuples by
texts.text: We require some notation. Fix a ∈ N . A tuple of polynomials ( P 1 , . . . , P a ) ∈ F q [ X ] a is said to be k -bounded if deg( P i ) < k for every 1 ≤ i ≤ a . We denote the set of all k -bounded tuples by

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: Q k,a = { ( P 1 , . . . , P a ) ∈ F q [ X ] a | ∀ 1 ≤ i ≤ a, deg( P i ) < k } .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Observe that Q k,a is an F q -linear space. If S ⊆ F q [ X ] a consists of k -bounded tuples, we say that S itself is k -bounded .
texts.text: Observe that Q k,a is an F q -linear space. If S ⊆ F q [ X ] a consists of k -bounded tuples, we say that S itself is k -bounded .

For S ⊆ F q [ X ] a , we denote S ( α ) := { P ( α ) | P ∈ S } . If A ∈ F q [ X ] m × m ′ is a matrix of polynomials, let A ( α ) ∈ F m × m ′ q denote the entry-wise evaluation matrix of A on α .
texts.text: For α ∈ F q and P = ( P 1 , . . . , P a ) ∈ F q [ X ] a , we denote the evaluation P P ( α ) := ( P 1 ( α ) , . . . , P a ( α )). For S ⊆ F q [ X ] a , we denote S ( α ) := { P ( α ) | P ∈ S } . If A ∈ F q [ X ] m × m ′ is a matrix of polynomials, let A ( α ) ∈ F m × m ′ q denote the entry-wise evaluation matrix of A on α .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let F q ( X ) denote the field of univariate rational functions over F q . We naturally embed F q in F q ( X ) by considering each α ∈ F q as the constant rational function α ∈ F q ( X ). By abuse of notation, both objects will be denoted by α . Note that every F q ( X )-linear space is also F q -linear. Throughout this section we use dim and Dim to refer to linear dimension of F q and F q ( X ), respectively. We similarly distinguish between span and Span, and between rank and Rank. Observe that the space Q k,a is F q -linear but not F q ( X )-linear.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 7.2. An F q ( X ) -linear map ψ : F q ( X ) b → F q ( X ) a is said to be polynomial if ψ ( F q [ X ] b ) ⊆ F q [ X ] a . In other words, ψ is polynomial if it is represented in the standard basis by a matrix with polynomial entries. We use deg ψ to denote the maximum degree (as a polynomial) of an entry in this matrix.
texts.text: Definition 7.2. An F q ( X ) -linear map ψ : F q ( X ) b → F q ( X ) a is said to be polynomial if ψ ( F q [ X ] b ) ⊆ F q [ X ] a . In other words, ψ is polynomial if it is represented in the standard basis by a matrix with polynomial entries. We use deg ψ to denote the maximum degree (as a polynomial) of an entry in this matrix.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 7.3. A b -local polynomial profile is a sequence Ψ = ( ψ 1 , . . . ψ n ) of F q ( X ) -linear polynomial maps ψ i : F q ( X ) b → F q ( X ) b i (for some b 1 , . . . , b n ∈ N ). We denote deg Ψ = max 1 ≤ i ≤ n deg ψ i .
texts.text: Definition 7.3. A b -local polynomial profile is a sequence Ψ = ( ψ 1 , . . . ψ n ) of F q ( X ) -linear polynomial maps ψ i : F q ( X ) b → F q ( X ) b i (for some b 1 , . . . , b n ∈ N ). We denote deg Ψ = max 1 ≤ i ≤ n deg ψ i .

texts.orig: Polynomial profiles provide a framework for directly analyzing and manipulating message vectors, which, in the case of random RS codes, correspond to the coefficient vectors of low-degree polynomials. This generalization enables one to define profiles that are well-suited for the stochastic process to proceed until completion (see Section 7.4 for further details). Additionally, we require a notion that is related to the concept of Φ , which is defined for subspaces of F b q .
texts.text: Polynomial profiles provide a framework for directly analyzing and manipulating message vectors, which, in the case of random RS codes, correspond to the coefficient vectors of low-degree polynomials. This generalization enables one to define profiles that are well-suited for the stochastic process to proceed until completion (see Section 7.4 for further details). Additionally, we require a notion that is related to the concept of Φ , which is defined for subspaces of F b q .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 7.4 (Strength) . For an F q ( X ) -linear subspace U ⊆ F q ( X ) b and a polynomial map ψ , we denote strength U ( ψ ) := Dim ψ ( U ) . Note that when U = F q ( X ) b , we have strength U ( ψ ) = Rank ψ .
texts.text: Definition 7.4 (Strength) . For an F q ( X ) -linear subspace U ⊆ F q ( X ) b and a polynomial map ψ , we denote strength U ( ψ ) := Dim ψ ( U ) . Note that when U = F q ( X ) b , we have strength U ( ψ ) = Rank ψ .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For an F q ( X ) -linear subspace U ⊆ F q ( X ) b and a polynomial profile Ψ = ( ψ 1 , . . . ψ n ) , we denote strength U (Ψ) = ∑ n i =1 strength U ( ψ i ) .
texts.text: For an F q ( X ) -linear subspace U ⊆ F q ( X ) b and a polynomial profile Ψ = ( ψ 1 , . . . ψ n ) , we denote strength U (Ψ) = ∑ n i =1 strength U ( ψ i ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Definition 7.5. Given T, U ⊆ F q ( X ) b , where U is an F q ( X ) -linear subspace, we say that U is T -live if Span( T ∩ U ) = U . Otherwise, we say that U is T -dead .
texts.text: Definition 7.5. Given T, U ⊆ F q ( X ) b , where U is an F q ( X ) -linear subspace, we say that U is T -live if Span( T ∩ U ) = U . Otherwise, we say that U is T -dead .

texts.orig: We turn to the examine the opposite case, namely, when Π W ( S, Ψ) is very negative (that is, much smaller than zero). Suppose that α is sampled uniformly at random from F n q . One may hope to show that S [Ψ , α ] ∩ W = { 0 } holds in this setting with high probability. However, this is not always true. Indeed, a construction along the lines of Example 4.2 yields a setting in which Π W ( S, Ψ) is very negative, but Π W ′ ( S, Ψ) is positive for some nontrivial F q ( X )-linear subspace W ′ ⊆ W . By Eq. (31), we then have S [Ψ , α ] ∩ W ⊇ S [Ψ , α ] ∩ W ′ /supersetnoteql { 0 } .

An equivalent condition for S n = { 0 } , is that every F q ( X )-linear space U ⊆ F q ( X ) b is S n -dead. Eq. (34) and Lemma 7.8 imply that this is very likely for any given U , so it seems reasonable to attempt a union bound over all S -live subspaces U (an analogous union bound over subspaces is inherent to our proof of Proposition 4.3). Unfortunately, the number of S -live F q ( X )-linear subspaces (roughly q Ω ( k · b 2 ) ) is too large for this approach to work. Therefore, we require a subtler strategy.

texts.orig: In our proof of Lemma 7.10, we follow the run of the iterative stochastic process described at the opening of this subsection. We run the process until a time s in which one of two stopping conditions occurs. The first condition is that the space W (namely, the F q ( X )-span of S ) is S s -dead, at which point we continue the run with regard to S s , whose F q ( X )-span is now smaller. This is the simple case.
texts.text: In our proof of Lemma 7.10, we follow the run of the iterative stochastic process described at the opening of this subsection. We run the process until a time s in which one of two stopping conditions occurs. The first condition is that the space W (namely, the F q ( X )-span of S ) is S s -dead, at which point we continue the run with regard to S s , whose F q ( X )-span is now smaller. This is the simple case.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The second stopping condition is that some F q ( X )-subspace U ⊆ W has become 'worrisome'. By this, we mean that p U,s := Π U ( S s , Ψ s +1 ··· n ) has increased and gotten too close to zero from the negative side. This is indeed worrying since, if this potential becomes positive, it is guaranteed that S n ∩ U = { 0 } , so we cannot hope to have S n = { 0 } . In this case, we proceed recursively by analyzing two different runs. First, we consider the set S s ∩ U and show the remaining negative potential p U,t is still negative enough to ensure that S n ∩ U = { 0 } with high probability. Second, we define a 'quotient set' S/U and 'quotient profile' Ψ /U . Using the fact that p U,s is only slightly negative, we show that this quotient run likely leads to the { 0 } -set, meaning that S n ⊆ U is likely. Together, S n ∩ U = { 0 } and S n ⊆ U imply that S n = { 0 } .

This is indeed worrying since, if this potential becomes positive, it is guaranteed that S n ∩ U = { 0 } , so we cannot hope to have S n = { 0 } . In this case, we proceed recursively by analyzing two different runs. First, we consider the set S s ∩ U and show the remaining negative potential p U,t is still negative enough to ensure that S n ∩ U = { 0 } with high probability. Second, we define a 'quotient set' S/U and 'quotient profile' Ψ /U . Using the fact that p U,s is only slightly negative, we show that this quotient run likely leads to the { 0 } -set, meaning that S n ⊆ U is likely. Together, S n ∩ U = { 0 } and S n ⊆ U imply that S n = { 0 } .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A Random Code Models
texts.text: A Random Code Models

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A.1 Models of Random Linear Codes
texts.text: A.1 Models of Random Linear Codes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this work, a random linear code of rate R is the kernel C of a uniformly random matrix P ∈ F ( n -k ) × n q . We note that if P happens not to have full rank, the code C would have rate larger than R . An arguably more accurate way to interpret the notion of random linear code is to sample a code C ′ uniformly at random from among all linear subspace of F n q of dimension Rn . However, the former model is usually nicer to work with because it satisfies the elegant Lemma 4.1. We justify our use of the former model rather than the latter by the following observation.
texts.text: In this work, a random linear code of rate R is the kernel C of a uniformly random matrix P ∈ F ( n -k ) × n q . We note that if P happens not to have full rank, the code C would have rate larger than R . An arguably more accurate way to interpret the notion of random linear code is to sample a code C ′ uniformly at random from among all linear subspace of F n q of dimension Rn . However, the former model is usually nicer to work with because it satisfies the elegant Lemma 4.1. We justify our use of the former model rather than the latter by the following observation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Lemma A.1. Write k = Rn . Let C be the kernel of a uniformly random matrix P ∈ F ( n -k ) × n q . Let C be a uniformly random subspace of F n q of dimension k . Then, the statistical difference between C and C ′ is at most 1 -e -q -Rn · n .
texts.text: Lemma A.1. Write k = Rn . Let C be the kernel of a uniformly random matrix P ∈ F ( n -k ) × n q . Let C be a uniformly random subspace of F n q of dimension k . Then, the statistical difference between C and C ′ is at most 1 -e -q -Rn · n .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. It is a well known fact that P has full rank with probability

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The lemma follows since, conditioned on P having full rank, C and C ′ have the same distribution.
texts.text: The lemma follows since, conditioned on P having full rank, C and C ′ have the same distribution.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: A.2 Models of Random RS Codes
texts.text: A.2 Models of Random RS Codes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For us, a random RS code is a code C = RS F q (( α 1 , . . . , α n ); k ) where α 1 , . . . , α n are sampled independently and uniformly from F q . Consider now the random code C ′ = RS F q (( β 1 , . . . , β n ); k ) where β 1 , . . . , β n are sampled uniformly from F n q without repetitions . While we have chosen to work with the former model out of convenience, we show that our results about list-decodability and list-recoverability essentially apply to the latter model as well. In fact, any result about the geometry of C also holds approximately for C ′ . This is due to the following lemma, which gives a coupling between the two models such that there exists an approximately distance preserving map between the two, provided that n q is small enough.
texts.text: For us, a random RS code is a code C = RS F q (( α 1 , . . . , α n ); k ) where α 1 , . . . , α n are sampled independently and uniformly from F q . Consider now the random code C ′ = RS F q (( β 1 , . . . , β n ); k ) where β 1 , . . . , β n are sampled uniformly from F n q without repetitions . While we have chosen to work with the former model out of convenience, we show that our results about list-decodability and list-recoverability essentially apply to the latter model as well. In fact, any result about the geometry of C also holds approximately for C ′ . This is due to the following lemma, which gives a coupling between the two models such that there exists an approximately distance preserving map between the two, provided that n q is small enough.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Consider a balls and bin scenario in which n balls are each thrown into one of q bins with uniform probability. Let X denote the number of non-empty bins. Note that | I | is distributed identically to n -X . Let X 1 , . . . , X q be variables such that X i takes 1 if the i -th bin is full, and 0 if it is empty. Note that E [ X i ] = 1 -( 1 -1 q ) n ≥ 1 -e -n q , so E [ X ] ≥ q · ( 1 -e -n q ) .
texts.text: Consider a balls and bin scenario in which n balls are each thrown into one of q bins with uniform probability. Let X denote the number of non-empty bins. Note that | I | is distributed identically to n -X . Let X 1 , . . . , X q be variables such that X i takes 1 if the i -th bin is full, and 0 if it is empty. Note that E [ X i ] = 1 -( 1 -1 q ) n ≥ 1 -e -n q , so E [ X ] ≥ q · ( 1 -e -n q ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Observe that X 1 , . . . , X q are in negative association (see [Waj17]). Indeed, by [Waj17, Theorem 10], the occupancy numbers, indicating the number of balls in each bin, are in negative association. Since X 1 , . . . , X q are monotone-increasing functions of the occupancy numbers, the relevant closure property [Waj17, Lemma 9], implies that X 1 , . . . , X q are also negatively associated. Thus, a Chernoff-Hoeffding bound [Waj17, Theorem 5] applies to their sum. Thus,
